title: After-LCM (Laser-Capture Microdissection) procedure

steps: After completing the LCM intended, lift the cap as instructed by the manufacturer, and assess the completeness of the capture by inspecting both the microdissected tissue attached to the cap, as well as the section dissected. NOTE : If debris or nonspecific tissue is adhered to the cap, gently press the cap onto a post-it note to eliminate contamination source.

Use the insertion tool (Arcturus LCM Cap Insertion Tool, Applied Biosystems, CA, USA) to transfer the cap from the LCM device to a 0.5-ml RNase-free microcentrifuge tube (N8010611, Applied BioSystems, CA, USA) containing 50 µL of lysis buffer (Arcturus® PicoPure RNA Isolation Kit, Applied Biosystems, CA, USA) in accordance to the Macro Cap protocol of the manufacturer (http:// tools.lifetechnologies.com/content/sfs/manuals /1268200.pdf).

Invert the cap–tube assembly, flick to ensure complete coverage of the cap surface by the extraction buffer. Incubate at 42 °C for 00:30:00 using the preheated incubation block, and then spin briefly to collect cell extract.

Discard the cap, save the tube with the cell extract, and immediately freeze the extract at -80 °C . Critical Step : RNA should be immediately extracted after LCM completion, and the microdissected cells should be collected in a contamination-free and non-contact manner.

Pooling : Combine the extracts from homogeneous cell populations captured on four caps, using the MCC tissues of the same animal, onto a single RNA purification column to achieve a sufficient yield of RNA. NOTE : Each LCM sample is prepared by pooling the cell extract of 4 caps containing microdissected cells procured from the same zone in 24 tissue sections mounted on 4 slides (6 sections/slide). However, 8 caps are pooled together to prepare one LCM sample of FZ as this zone is very small in size.
In accordance to the manufacturer’s instructions, first mix the pooled cell extract with 70% ethanol, and load onto a preconditioned purification column.
Spin the column and wash it twice with wash buffer.

Then, elute the RNA. However, some modifications are recommended: adjustment of the 70% ethanol volume to compensate for the total volume of the pooled sample ( 200 µL ), resulting in a combined volume of the cell extract/ethanol mix of approximately 400 µL . Since the loading capacity of the columns used is 300 µL , the cell extract/ethanol mix is transferred in two steps where 200 µL is loaded in each step. On-column DNase treatment (RNase-Free DNase Set, Qiagen GmbH, Hilden, Germany) is done regularly for all samples as the elimination of genomic DNA is critical for accurate downstream applications. This is done between the two washes of the column, and according to the manufacturer’s protocol. Also, the additional 00:01:00 re-centrifugation of the column (to ensure removal of the wash buffer before elution step) is regularly carried out.

For minute LCM-RNA samples, conventional methods applied to assess RNA quality have a tendency to produce inconsistent and imprecise results. Alternatively, qRT-PCR analysis to calculate the 3′/ m ratio for housekeeping genes is suggested as a metric measure to evaluate qualitative performance of small RNA samples. To measure the 3'/m ratio of GAPDH and β-Actin, firstly design two primers sets one specific for the 3'end, and the other one for the middle (m) region of each housekeeping gene (Table 1). Table 1 Sequence of primer sets for housekeeping genes used for qRT-PCR to evaluate LCM-RNA integrity. %s %s

Use isolated total RNA samples as a template to synthesize first-strand cDNA according to the manufacturer's instructions via reverse transcription process, using Superscript III Reverse Transcriptase (Invitrogen, CA, USA) and oligo (dT) [12–18] (Invitrogen, CA, USA) (http:// tools.lifetechnologies.com/content/sfs/manuals/superscriptIII_man.pdf). 10 µL to 11 µL of each LCM-RNA samples is used per 20 µL cDNA synthesis reaction using Veriti 96 well thermal cycler (Applied Biosystems, CA, USA).

For each primer pair, generate a standard curve with serial dilutions of cDNA synthesized by reverse transcription of total RNA isolated from a mixture of MCC specimens in accordance with the manufacturer’s instructions (RNeasy Fibrous Tissue Mini Kit, Qiagen, GmbH, Hilden, Germany) ( http://www.qiagen .com/us/resources/ resourcedetail?id=8039840d-4815-4375-8933-bc09247e47c0 &lang =en).

Perform qRT-PCR using a Step One Plus RT PCR system (Applied Biosystems, CA, USA) and Power SYBR® Green PCR master mix (Applied Biosystems, Warrington, UK). The parameters of thermal cycle are set as follows: 95 °C for 00:10:00 , 40 cycles of denaturation at 95 °C for 00:00:15 and annealing and extension at 60 °C for 00:01:00 .
To generate dissociation curves the reactions are denaturation at 95 °C for 00:00:15 annealing at 60 °C for 00:01:00 , followed by a gradual increase in temperature (0.3% ramp rate) to 95 °C .

Also generate melting curves to assess PCR specificity. Determine relative quantity for the four tested regions by utilizing the corresponding standard curves generated in the same experiment, and then the 3'/ m ratio is calculated for each sample. NOTE : The 3′/m ratio for a gene measures the abundance of that gene at the 3′ end to the abundance at the midregion. In the presence of nondegraded full-length transcripts, the two quantities at the 3′ and midregions are almost equal, resulting in a ratio close to 1, whereas shortened/degraded transcripts show less PCR amplification at (m) region and hence higher 3′/m ratio (Kube et al. 2007) There are no fixed cut-off values for the 3′/m ratio, as different studies could tolerate different ratios, however, in general a cut-off value of ≤2 have been chosen.
Apply the LCM protocol described above to collect total RNA from the four zones of MCC individually.
Then, prepare samples that meet quality requirements for subsequent molecular analyses.

In case of microarray analysis, use LCM-RNA samples as input material for a two-cycle linear amplification process, producing amplified aRNA samples which are then Biotin-labeled and fragmented before performing microarray hybridization.

Use 11 µL of each total RNA sample as input material for a two-cycle linear amplification process in accordance to the protocol provided with the Arcturus® RiboAmp® HS Plus amplification Kit (Applied Biosystems, CA, USA) ( https://tools.lifetechnologies.com/ content/sfs/manuals/cms_085206.pdf ), but with only one modification in the last step of the protocol (aRNA purification).
Concurrently, amplify a 10 µL sample ( 500 pg of total RNA) from the RNA control provided with the kit to verify the efficiency of amplification.
Perform two cycles of linear amplification, each cycle consists of five steps:

First strand synthesis reaction : Add 1 µL of oligo(dT) primer incorporating a T7 promoter sequence to the total RNA sample (approx. 11μl), then incubate for 00:05:00 at 65 °C . Follow this by adding the first strand cDNA components provided with the kit, together with SuperScript™ III Reverse Transcriptase (Invitrogen, CA, USA), and incubating the mix for 01:00:00 at 42 °C . At this point, remove 2 µL of each sample to assess the integrity of the starting mRNA by evaluating the 3'/m ratio of GAPDH and β- Actin housekeeping genes via the qRT-PCR assay as described in section "Evaluation of RNA Integrity".

Second strand synthesis reaction : Add 1 µL of exogenous primer, followed by incubation for 00:02:00 at 95 °C . Then, add second strand cDNA components and incubate as instructed to produce double-stranded cDNA.
cDNA purification : Use MiraCol™ purification columns and wash the cDNA. Elute it with the buffers provided.

In vitro transcription : Add IVT components including T7 RNA polymerase, followed by incubation for 06:00:00 at 42 °C using Veriti 96 well thermal cycler (Applied Biosystems, CA, USA) to yield antisense RNA (aRNA).

aRNA purification : After performing the DNase step according to the manufacturer’s instructions, use MiraCol purification columns (together with the buffers provided) to purify the resulting amplified aRNA, and eventually elute it in 12 µL volume of the elution buffer.

In the second cycle of amplification , incubate the purified product of the first cycle with random primers for 00:05:00 at 65 °C . Then utilize SuperScript III Reverse Transcriptase to synthesize first-strand cDNA. Carry out the remaining four processes of the second cycle as described in the kit protocol with only one modification. Reduce the volume of the eluting buffer from 30 µL to 15 µL , followed by a second elution step utilizing another 15 µL and another tube. NOTE : This is recommended to obtain higher cRNA concentrations during the first elution, thus eliminating the need to concentrate diluted samples (if any) with vacuum centrifuge. After that, quality control measurements are employed to determine both the mRNA transcript length using Agilent 2100 Bioanalyzer with a total RNA 6000 Nano-lab-Chip (Agilent technologies, CA, USA), as well as RNA purity via a NanoDrop 2000c spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific) where a ratio of two optical densities; A260 and A280 is calculated. 2 µL of each sample is diluted to 6 µL , and then divided into two aliquots of 3 µL . One for the Bioanalyzer profile, and the other aliquot is used to measure sample concentration and purity (OD 260/280) using the Nanodrop.

Calculate amplification efficiency of the performed linear amplification by dividing the yield of the RNA control sample after the amplification step by the initial RNA input. Utilize the remaining 13 µL of the first elution for labeling and microarray hybridization, while the products of second elution are stored at -70 °C .

Perform Labeling of aRNA transcripts using the Arcturus® Turbo Labeling™ Biotin Reagent Kit (Applied Biosystems, Amsterdam, The Netherlands) and in accordance to the manufacturer’s instructions ( https://tools.lifetechnologies.com/ content/sfs/manuals/cms_085525.pd f ), but with minor modifications.
Firstly, increase the amount of aRNA input to 25 µg instead of 1-20 µg range recommended by the manufacturer.

Secondly, add extra 5 µL of nuclease-free water to the same column after the first elution of labeled aRNA (as recommended by Pietersen et al. 2009), so that the final volume is 25 µL . NOTE : These modifications are considered to ensure the availability of enough labeled aRNA quantities for the subsequent fragmentation and hybridization procedure, also, to account for the volumes needed for quantifying the yielded labeled aRNA, and for assessing the quality of fragmented product later on.
The protocol for labeling, in brief, is as the following:
Adjust the volume of 25 µg of each aRNA sample to a total of 13 µL . Then, prepare the reaction mix for labeling ( 20 µL in total) and incubate as described in the protocol of the kit.
Resuspend the columns provided with the kit and wash it as instructed.

Remove the excess/free biotin label, and then add 5 µL of nuclease-free water to the same column, followed by centrifugation for 00:01:00 , producing a final volume of 25 µL . Quantify the amount of biotin-labeled aRNA using the Nanodrop.

Critical : Fragmentation of aRNA is critical for microarray hybridization. The amount of aRNA needed depends on the GeneChip array format, for instance, the 64 Array Format needs 15 μg of aRNA. NOTE: Fragmentation buffer (5X) of the GeneChip® 3' IVT Express Kit (Affymetrix, Santa Clara, CA, USA) is used to prepare the samples. The protocol followed is in the accordance to the Arcturus® Turbo Labeling™ Kit with Biotin manufacturer’s instructions (https:// tools.lifetechnologies.com/content/sfs/manuals/cms_085525.pdf), but with modification to the total fragmentation mix volume. Increase the total volume from 25 µL ( 15 µg labeled aRNA in 20 µL + 5 µL of 5X fragmentation buffer) to 28 µL ( 16.8 µg labeled aRNA in 22.4 µL + 5.6 µL of 5X fragmentation buffer) while maintaining a 4:1 ratio of the two components of the mix. This is to account for the volume needed for the optional Bioanalyzer evaluation to analyze the size of the products after incubating the mix at 94 °C for 00:10:00 , while an aliquot of 12.5 µg of fragmented and labeled aRNA in 25 µL is saved for preparation of hybridization mix. NOTE: According to the protocol of Affymetrix GeneChip® 3' IVT Express Kit ( http://media.affymetrix.com/support/downloads /manuals/ht_3_ivt_express_kit_manual.pdf), the total volume of the hybridization mix for 64 array format is 250 µL , while it is 300 µL in the protocol of Applied Biosystems labeling Kit (https:// tools.lifetechnologies.com/content/sfs/manuals/cms_085525.pdf). In addition, the latter protocol recommended to add a turbo blocking reagent (provided with the kit) to reduce the fluorescence background on the arrays. To resolve this discrepancy, we integrated the two protocols with the help of the technical support section of Applied Biosystems (Table 2). Table 2 Hybridization mix of Affymetrix protocol, Applied Biosystems protocol, and our integrated protocol. %s %s

The prepared hybridization mix containing fragmented and labeled aRNA ( 12.5 µg in 25 µL each) is now ready for microarray hybridization using GeneChip Rat Genome 230 2.0 Array (Affymetrix, Santa Clara, CA, USA).
creator: Aisha Basudan
